text: In a laparoscopic view, a 23-gauge needle is inserted intraluminally for ICG injection at the two, five, and seven o'clock positions around the tumor. The anus is tightly closed, and infrared laparoscopy reveals ICG fluorescence diffused around the rectum. The surgeon mobilizes the left-sided colon and performs high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery.

The hypogastric nerve is visible as the surgeon proceeds with the transperineal approach. A skin incision is made around the tightly closed anus, and a gel point mini is connected to initiate the approach using three ports, including a camera port. The operative field direction relative to the anus is displayed in the lower left corner of the video. Fat tissues in the ischioanal fossa are divided, exposing the levator ani muscle. Posterior dissection begins, dividing the levator ani muscle just above the coccyx from the posterior to bilateral sides. ICG fluorescence appears, aiding in identifying the dissection plane amidst exudate fluid from preoperative radiotherapy.

The surgeon pushes up the posterior side of the rectum, identifying and dividing the fibrous connective tissues covering the mesorectum using electric cautery. The dissection continues posteriorly towards the sacral promontory, avoiding the hypogastric nerve. The dissection extends laterally, where the pelvic splanchnic nerves are identified at the five o'clock position, serving as landmarks. Anterior dissection is performed just behind the transverse perineal muscle.

On the right anterior side, the surgeon uses electric cautery to divide the levator ani muscle, exposing the ICG-stained endopelvic fascia. The green color of the ICG serves as a landmark for the fascia. After dividing the green-stained fascia, the neurovascular bundle is identified.

The surgeon identifies the arms of the puborectalis sling at the one o'clock position and divides the puborectalis muscle using electric cautery. Dissection near the neurovascular bundle results in minor bleeding, and the ICG-stained endopelvic fascia is visible. The smooth muscle is cut, and adjustments to the dissection line are made due to bleeding points and increased fibrosis from preoperative chemoradiotherapy. The neurovascular bundle serves as a landmark for the dissection line, and the proper dissection plane behind the prostate is identified, facilitating continuation along this plane. Anterior dissection extends to the lateral side while preserving the neurovascular bundle, continuing until reaching the pouch of Douglas.

The surgeon extends the dissection between the mesorectum and neurovascular bundle bilaterally from the transperineal approach, revealing the perineal body, neurovascular bundle, and cut end of the levator ani muscle. The view shifts to the abdominal approach, showing the left, anterior, and right sides. Rectal mobilization is completed, and the resected specimen is extracted through the anus.
